content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 13,
1994
Margaret M.
Lauerman,
CMC
City Clerk
P.
0.
Box 6459
Burbank,
CA
91510-6459
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1-94-259
Dear Ms.
Lauerman:
You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) •1/
Because it does not
appear that you are seeking this advice as the authorized
representative of the candidate,
we will treat your letter as a
request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) 2/
OUESTION
A member of the Burbank City Council filed a short form
campaign statement
(Form 470) with the County of Los Angeles in
connection with her candidacy for the 43rd District County Central
Committee.
You have asked if the councilmember was also required
to file an originally signed copy of the Form 470 with your
office.
CONCLUSION
When an individual holds one elective office and runs for
another office in a different jurisdiction, the individual and his
or her controlled committees must file campaign statements in both
jurisdictions.
Therefore, during the time the councilmember is
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Margaret Lauerman
Our File No.
1—94—259
Page
2
both a city officeholder and a candidate for county office, both
the city and the county are filing officers for all of her
campaign statements.
Although the Cominission has advised that original statements
should be filed with each filing officer,
as long as the
originally signed Form 470 in connection with the county election
was properly filed with the county,
we see no public harm in the
city accepting a duplicate of the originally signed file-stamped
form on file with the county.
ANALYSIS
In its statement of purposes, the Act provides:
Receipts and expenditures in election campaigns
should be fully and truthfully disclosed in order
that the voters may be fully informed and improper
practices may be inhibited.
Section 81002(a).
(Emphasis added.)
Section 84215(d) requires county officeholders and candidates
for county offices to file campaign statements in the county.
Section 84215(e) requires city officeholders and candidates for
city offices to file campaign statements in the city.
The
Commission has advised that an elected officeholder who runs for
an office in a different jurisdiction must file all campaign
statements required for both offices in both jurisdictions.
(Barrett Advice Letter,
No. A-88-150; Fishburn Advice Letter,
No.
1—91—325.)
Although we have also advised that originally signed campaign
statements should be filed in both jurisdictions, it appears that
in this case, there will be no public harm if the city filing
officer receives a reproduction of the candidate’s originally
signed short form campaign statement which is on file with the
county filing officer in connection with the county election.
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
